Newborns can only see about 8-12 inches away — just far enough to see the face of whoever is holding them.
Babies are born with about 300 bones, but adults only have 206. Many bones fuse together as they grow!
A newborn's stomach is only about the size of a marble on day one.
Babies can recognize their mother's voice from birth — they've been listening for months in the womb!
Newborns have an automatic stepping reflex. Hold them upright with feet touching a surface and they'll make walking motions.
A baby's brain doubles in size during the first year of life.
Babies are born without kneecaps! They develop between 3-5 years of age.
Newborns can taste sweet, sour, and bitter flavors, but can't taste salt until about 5 months.
Babies have a natural diving reflex — if submerged in water, they instinctively hold their breath and slow their heart rate.
Newborns spend about 16-17 hours a day sleeping, but rarely more than 2-4 hours at a stretch.
A baby's grip strength is strong enough to support their own body weight. (Don't test this at home.)
Babies prefer to look at faces over any other pattern or shape.
The soft spot on a baby's head (fontanelle) allows the skull to compress during delivery and accommodates rapid brain growth.
Newborns cry without tears for the first few weeks — their tear ducts aren't fully developed yet.
Babies can hear and respond to music they heard frequently during pregnancy.
A newborn's heart beats about 130-160 times per minute — roughly twice as fast as an adult's.
Skin-to-skin contact right after birth helps regulate a baby's temperature, heart rate, and breathing.
Babies are born with a natural preference for sweet tastes — breast milk is naturally sweet!
The average newborn weighs about 7.5 pounds, but healthy babies range from 5.5 to 10 pounds.
Newborns can smell their mother's breast milk from across the room within the first week of life.
